title: GB total-miss fallback not implemented (PR #81 spec gap)
description:
Manual test of PR #81 on 2026-05-16 surfaced that the GB total-miss fallback case is broken. ISBN 9780593875490 (a new book not in Open Library) returns 'fill in manually' instead of falling back to Google Books.

Root cause: FetchOpenLibraryBook returns ErrOpenLibraryNotFound IMMEDIATELY when entry not found in OL payload (around line 300), BEFORE any goroutine launches. The GB goroutine is only added inside the parallel fan-out section after the OL entry is confirmed present. So GB never fires when OL has no record at all.

Spec says: 'Goal: total-miss fallback plus field-level enrichment over partial OL hits' (docs/specs/2026-05-15-google-books-fallback-design.md line 145). The enrichment-over-partial-hits half works (verified by Rule of Four test); the total-miss fallback half does not.

Opus final review of PR #81 caught this in Minor #4 ('Missing test: GB never fires when OL returns ErrOpenLibraryNotFound. The code returns the sentinel at line 307 before the goroutine block.') -- I read it as a missing-test concern, not a spec violation. The test was missing because the feature was missing.

Fix: when OL returns no record and GB is configured, attempt FetchByISBN before returning ErrOpenLibraryNotFound. On GB success, return mergePrefill(nil, gbResult) which produces a GB-only prefill with source labels stamped. On GB not-found or disabled or error, fall through to the existing ErrOpenLibraryNotFound return (preserving today's behavior for the never-GB case).

Adding to PR #81 as another commit on feat/google-books-fallback so the PR ships a complete Subproject A.

title: Settings env-var precedence: LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE silently overridden by stale DB row
description:
## Problem

The current offline-mode precedence (per DEC-033) is: DB row wins over env var. That is the documented design and the right call for the in-flight 'temporarily flip offline during a network outage without restart' use case.

But it has a UX trap that bit Tim during manual A0 testing on 2026-05-15:

1. Admin starts the server WITHOUT LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE set (default).
2. Admin visits /admin/settings and clicks Save while exploring, without changing the offline_mode checkbox. Because HandleSettingsPost writes every known setting on every POST (treating absent checkboxes as 'off'), this writes offline_mode=false to the settings table.
3. Admin later restarts the server with LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE=true expecting offline mode. The DB row (false) wins over the env var (true). OL Lookup returns 200 instead of 503. No error, no warning, no log line indicating the env var was overridden.

Tim's session log showed the symptom: OL Lookup at /api/openlibrary/isbn/9780141439518 took 1.01s and returned 200 with LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE=true clearly set on the go run invocation.

## Why this matters

The env-var-as-startup-default story (in deployment.md and DEC-033) is leaky. Operators reading the docs will believe LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE=true means 'offline.' In practice it only means 'offline on first launch of a fresh DB, before anyone clicks Save in Settings.' That gap is wide enough to cause real incidents -- an operator deploys to a prison expecting offline, an admin saves the settings form once on Day 1, six months later a Day-2 operator restarts with the env var and OL starts phoning home from inside the facility.

## Design options (pick during a brainstorm)

Option A -- Only write changed checkboxes
HandleSettingsPost only calls SetSetting for keys whose form value differs from the current DB value. Absent-but-unchanged stays absent. Pros: env var stays load-bearing forever; the Save-while-exploring case becomes a no-op. Cons: contradicts the documented design 'every POST is set-state-from-form' that the cross-toggle non-interference test pins (TestSettingsPagePOST_BothTogglesWrittenEveryPOST). Breaks that test contract.

Option B -- Show env-var-vs-DB precedence in the UI
Render the toggle with both states visible: 'DB: ON (active); env says OFF.' Operator sees the override visually and can clear the DB row from the UI. Pros: no precedence change, no design churn. Cons: dense UI; adds a 'Reset to env default' button which is more surface area.

Option C -- Reverse the precedence: env var wins when set
Env var becomes the override when explicitly set in the environment; DB row applies only when env var is unset. Pros: matches operator mental model ('I set the env var, it's authoritative'). Cons: removes the 'temporary outage flip without restart' use case the spec called out; the runtime toggle becomes useless when the env var is set.

Option D -- Hybrid: env var wins on read AND POST refuses to write a value that contradicts the env var
Operator who explicitly set LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE=true cannot turn it off via the UI without first unsetting the env var and restarting. The toggle is greyed out with a tooltip. Pros: matches the 'declared offline = stays offline' intent for restricted-network deployments. Cons: most complex; needs UI affordance for the lock state.

Tim leans toward A or D pending brainstorm. Option B is the safest patch but might be the wrong primitive long-term.

## Discovery and impact

- Filed 2026-05-15 during PR #78 manual test (cs408-go-stack-ahq, branch feat/offline-mode-a0).
- Pre-merge: this is a latent UX bug in A0 that will hit again when Subproject A (Google Books) lands, because GB reads the same predicate.
- Recommended sequencing: brainstorm + fix BEFORE A merges. Updating the docs alone is insufficient -- the deployment.md text already says the settings row wins, and operators still get surprised.

## Tests to add after fix

- Whichever option lands, write a test that captures the new contract explicitly. For Option A: 'POSTing without offline_mode in the form does not write offline_mode' (currently the opposite is asserted). For Option D: 'when env says offline, POST cannot write offline_mode=false.'
- Update deployment.md and DEC-033 to match the new behavior. The current DEC-033 'Why not auto-detect' rationale is unchanged; the precedence subsection needs a rewrite.
notes: Merged via PR #79 on 2026-05-15. DEC-034 published to main, supersedes DEC-033 precedence subsection. 7 commits TDD-ordered (predicate + handler/template + startup log + docs), 2 follow-up commits from in-task code review (doc tightening + test assertion tightening). Manual test plan completed by Tim. Closes the precedence trap that bit during PR #78 manual test.

title: Gate SaveCoverFromURL with IsExternalAllowed predicate
description:
Final code review of feat/offline-mode-a0 flagged that SaveCoverFromURL (covers.go:99) is reachable from HandleBookCreate (handlers_books.go:274) and HandleBookUpdate (handlers_books.go:500) without checking the offline-mode predicate.

The path is via the cover_url hidden form field which JS populates after a successful OL Lookup. In normal offline-mode flow that field stays empty because OL Lookup itself returns 503, so practical exposure is narrow. But a stale tab opened before going offline, or a manual form replay, would still trigger an outbound HTTP fetch to whatever URL is in the field.

Pre-existing on main (not a regression introduced by A0). Spec wording 'no external HTTP calls are made by OL Lookup, the seed-cover backfill, or any future external source' does not unambiguously cover cover-by-URL.

Recommendation: Gate SaveCoverFromURL or its two callers with IsExternalAllowed. Reject the submit or strip the cover URL on the handler side when offline. Add corresponding handler tests.

Sequencing: do BEFORE Subproject A (Google Books) merges, since A's merge logic will force a fresh audit of all external-HTTP call sites anyway.
notes: Merged via PR #80 on 2026-05-15. With this in, the call-site audit for DEC-033 + DEC-034 is complete; all three external-HTTP entry points (FetchOpenLibraryBookGated, FetchAndStoreSeedCovers, SaveCoverFromURLGated) now respect the offline-mode predicate. Subproject A (Google Books, cs408-go-stack-8gj) can proceed without re-auditing the existing surface.

title: OL Lookup: revert Wikipedia, use OL work record for descriptions + jscmd=data for authors
description:
The Wikipedia REST summary endpoint returns the article's LEAD section, which for most book articles is meta-content (about who wrote it / when published / awards), not jacket synopsis. Tim's example: 'The Rule of Four' returned 'The Rule of Four is a novel written by the American authors Ian Caldwell and Dustin Thomason, and published in 2004...' -- about the book, not OF the book.

OL itself has the right content -- in the work record, not the edition record. /works/OL.W.json for Rule of Four returns 'Princeton. Good Friday, 1999. On the eve of graduation, two students are a hairsbreadth from solving...' which IS the jacket synopsis. Same for Dune's work record.

Similarly, sparse editions are missing authors from jscmd=details but jscmd=data resolves them cleanly from the work refs (Rule of Four data shows Ian Caldwell, Dustin Thomason, Eiko Kakinuma by name).

Fix:
- Remove wikipedia.go entirely (and revert its handler integration + JS branch).
- When edition.description is empty/thin, follow works[0].key and fetch /works/X.json; use its description.
- When edition has no authors (neither structured 'authors' nor singular 'author'), call jscmd=data and use its resolved authors.

Keep DescriptionSource field for future Google Books integration (cs408-go-stack-8gj will benefit when OL has no record at all -- the small minority of books OL doesn't catalog).
notes:
Closed 2026-05-13. Pivoted away from Wikipedia for descriptions; both authors and descriptions now come entirely from OL, just with two additional OL endpoints chained behind the primary jscmd=details call.

Why the pivot: Wikipedia's REST /api/rest_v1/page/summary/<title> endpoint returns the article's LEAD section, which for most book articles is meta-content ('X is a 2004 novel by Y, published by Z, who graduated from Princeton...') -- not the jacket-style synopsis Tim was after. Tim's example: 'The Rule of Four' returned a paragraph about Caldwell and Thomason's biographies instead of a description of the actual book. Same shape for Dune.

The right content was hiding in OL itself, just on the work record rather than the edition record. /works/OL8990536W.json for Rule of Four returns 'Princeton. Good Friday, 1999. On the eve of graduation, two students are a hairsbreadth from solving the mysteries of the Hypnerotomachia Poliphili...' (literally back-cover text ending '--BOOK JACKET.'). Same for Dune's work record.

Similarly, sparse editions like the Dell paperback of Rule of Four have no authors on the edition record but jscmd=data resolves them from the work refs.

Implementation:
- Deleted wikipedia.go and wikipedia_test.go (~525 lines removed).
- Reverted handlers_books.go: HandleOpenLibraryLookup no longer calls Wikipedia. Kept DescriptionSource = 'openlibrary' since future Google Books integration (cs408-go-stack-8gj) will still want to distinguish sources.
- Reverted JS status banner to the simpler 'Prefilled from Open Library' form.
- openlibrary.go: added Author []string singular-form fallback (folded in cs408-go-stack-7iu) AND added two new chained fetches:
  - fetchOLWorkDescription(ctx, workKey): follows entry.Details.Works[0].Key to <host>/<key>.json and parses .description (reusing olDescription's string-or-{type,value} unmarshaler). Non-fatal on failure -- the edition metadata still flows back.
  - fetchOLDataAuthors(ctx, bibkey): makes a second /api/books call with jscmd=data when authors are still empty after parsing both edition shapes. Non-fatal on failure.

normalizeOLAuthorString flips OL's catalog-card form 'Austen, Jane, 1775-1817.' to 'Jane Austen': strips a trailing period only when the preceding char is a digit (so 'Tolkien, J. R. R.' survives intact), splits on the first comma, drops any trailing date or role suffix, and assembles 'First Last'.

Live verification against real OL on a fresh dev server:
- ISBN 9780440241355 (Rule of Four, Dell ed.): authors now ['Ian Caldwell', 'Dustin Thomason', 'Eiko Kakinuma'] (was []); description is the actual back-cover text ending '--BOOK JACKET.' (was Wikipedia meta-content).
- ISBN 9780441013593 (Dune, Ace ed.): description is the full back-cover synopsis 'Set on the desert planet Arrakis, Dune is the story of the boy Paul Atreides...' (was Wikipedia's '1965 novel by...' lead).
- ISBN 9780141439518 (Pride and Prejudice, Penguin Classics): unchanged -- OL edition already had the back-cover blurb, no fallback fired.

Tests: four new in openlibrary_test.go covering both fallbacks' happy paths and silent-on-404 contracts. Existing tests still pass; the fakeOLServer helper now swaps both openLibraryBaseURL and openLibraryHost so a stray fallback fetch can't accidentally hit real OL.

Security review: clean. URL concatenation in fetchOLWorkDescription is gated by a hardcoded openLibraryHost prefix; userinfo-injection-via-OL-record-edit is theoretically possible but the request is unauthenticated, no cookies, response body silently discarded -- below the 8/10 reporting threshold for the OL threat model.

Folded in: cs408-go-stack-7iu (singular 'author' field handling -- the Author []string parsing and normalizeOLAuthorString live in this same commit). Sets up: cs408-go-stack-8gj as a Google Books fallback for the small remaining set of books OL doesn't catalog at all. Supersedes: cs408-go-stack-g5x (Wikipedia descriptions) -- reverted because the content shape was wrong; leaving g5x closed since the work it represented is no longer wanted.

title: Flaky test: TestPatronLoginCredentials_RendersTempPasswordPage HTML-escape race
description: The test at handlers_patrons_test.go:419 asserts strings.Contains(body, temp) where temp is a random password drawn from an alphabet including '&'. When the random temp contains '&', html/template renders it as '&' in the page body, so the substring assertion fails. Reproduced locally with -count=50. The handler/template are correct; only the test assertion is wrong. Fix: compare against html.EscapeString(temp). Surfaced as a CI failure on commit 40129a3 (docs-only push) on 2026-05-10.

title: UX hazard: temp passwords unrecoverable after navigating away from import result page
description:
After a with-logins CSV import, the result page shows a single-use
"Download credentials CSV" link. The temp passwords live only in an
in-memory map keyed by a token embedded in that link. If the admin
navigates away without clicking, the plaintext temps are gone --
bcrypt-hashed in the users table, no recovery path short of wiping
the DB or manually resetting each password.

Discovered 2026-05-06 during manual testing of cs408-go-stack-c98
section 5.

Tim's preferred direction: temp passwords should be retrievable per-
patron from /patrons (inline reveal on the row), not as a single
all-at-once dump on the result page.

Architectural options proposed (full discussion in conversation
2026-05-06):

Shape 1: persist plaintext temp in a temp_password column on users,
         clear on first login OR admin dismissal, per-row reveal UI
Shape 2: regenerate per-patron on demand (no plaintext stored)
Shape 3: Shape 1 + AES encryption with app-key

Tim leaning toward Shape 1; decision deferred to a fresh session.

Three sub-questions to answer before locking design:
1. Auto-clear timing: only on patron's first successful login, or
   also when admin clicks "Mark as delivered"?
2. Reveal visibility: should any staff see any patron's temp via the
   reveal button, or restricted to admin / the user who imported?
3. Regenerate workflow: add a "Regenerate temp password" action so
   admins can recover from a lost-temp scenario?

Closes alongside the implementation; do not close cs408-go-stack-8ap
or cs408-go-stack-c98 until this is resolved.

title: Manual test plan: CSV patron import + force-change-on-first-login
description:
Manual smoke test for branch csv-patron-import. Run end-to-end before
the automated test pass and PR.

Parent issues: cs408-go-stack-8ap (CSV import), cs408-go-stack-rn0
(force-change Variant 2), cs408-go-stack-d0w (settings infrastructure),
cs408-go-stack-fut (per-row temp password recovery).

## Setup

```
git checkout csv-patron-import
git pull
rm -f data/database.sqlite*
go run .
```

Test CSVs already committed at tmp/inmates.csv and tmp/library.csv.

Default seeded accounts (created on first run):
- admin / Admin123!
- staff1 / Staff123!
- patron1 / Patron123!

## 1. Admin tools-index navigation
- /admin shows two cards: Backup and Restore, Settings (Patron Import
  was removed from /admin in favor of the /patrons entry point)
- Each card opens its tool with a working "Back to Admin"

## 2. Settings page
- Toggle "Allow staff to import patrons" defaults off
- Flip on, save -> green flash "Settings saved." -> persists across refresh
- Flip off, save, confirm persists

## 3. Patron import: records-only flow
- /patrons shows the "Import CSV" button next to "+ Add Patron"
- Click it; upload tmp/inmates.csv; leave "Patron records only" selected
- Preview: Insertable=3, EmptyName=1, columns mapped (name, external_id
  standard; Cell Block, Security Level metadata)
- Sample shows John, Jane, Robert with IDOC numbers in External ID
- Click "Import 3 patrons" -> result page "3 patrons were imported"
- /patrons shows all three with "no login" in the Username column. No
  "Login Setup" button (records-only patrons have no login)

## 4. Dedup behavior
- Re-upload tmp/inmates.csv same settings
- Preview: Insertable=0, Skipped (duplicate)=3, EmptyName=1
- Click Import; error report download lists "External ID matches existing
  patron"

## 5. With-logins flow + per-row reveal (NEW)
This section validates the temp-password persistence + recovery design.
Wipe and restart for a clean run:

```
rm -f data/database.sqlite*
go run .
```

Login as admin. /patrons -> Import CSV -> upload tmp/library.csv ->
select "Patron records with login accounts" -> Continue -> Import.

Result page assertions:
- Yellow Credentials card with download button labeled "Download
  credentials CSV (single use)"
- Below the download button, a tip mentions retrieving temp passwords
  from each patron's row on /patrons

DELIBERATELY do NOT click the download button. Click "Back to Admin" or
navigate elsewhere. (Earlier this was the failure mode that lost temps;
it should now be recoverable.)

Visit /patrons:
- Alice Brown, Bob Wilson, Charlie Davis are listed
- Each has a yellow "Login Setup" button in the Actions column
- Seed Patron does NOT (already has a known password)

Click "Login Setup" for Alice:
- Page shows: Patron name, Username (abrown), Temporary password
- Two buttons: "Mark as Delivered" (filled blue), "Regenerate Temporary
  Password" (outlined)
- Top-right "Back to Patrons" link
- (Devtools sanity check: Cache-Control: no-store on the response)

Save Alice's temp password somewhere (you'll use it in section 6).

Test "Mark as Delivered" for Alice:
- Click button -> redirects to /patrons with "Temporary password marked
  as delivered. Alice Brown" flash
- Alice's row no longer has the "Login Setup" button

Test "Regenerate" for Bob:
- Click "Login Setup" on Bob's row
- Save Bob's CURRENT temp password (call it BobTemp1)
- Click "Regenerate Temporary Password" -> page reloads with new temp
  visible (call it BobTemp2) and "New temporary password generated."
  flash
- BobTemp1 should now be invalid (test by trying to log in as bob with
  BobTemp1 in section 6 -- it should fail, while BobTemp2 should succeed)

Charlie's "Login Setup" stays as a control case (don't touch it).

## 6. Force-change-on-first-login flow
Use the temp passwords from section 5.

- Log out, log in as alice (Alice's "Login Setup" was dismissed in 5,
  so use the temp you saved)
- Should redirect to /account/change-password
- Banner: "Welcome. Please set a new password before continuing."
- Weak password -> red error
- Mismatched confirm -> red error
- Set a strong matching password (e.g. NewPass1!) -> redirect to /login
- Log in with abrown / NewPass1! -> normal dashboard
- /patrons (as admin) -> Alice's row now shows "abrown" in Username
  with NO "Login Setup" button (temp cleared by UpdateUserPassword)

## 7. Force-change escape hatch
Wipe DB and re-import tmp/library.csv with logins for a fresh setup.
Log in as one of the imported patrons with their temp pw.

- Try /catalog, /, /admin -> all redirect to /account/change-password
- Click "Cancel and sign out" -> logout works, lands at /login
- Log in again with the original temp pw -> still forced (flag persists
  across logout/login; temp_password row still set)

## 8. Settings + role gating
- Login as admin, /admin/settings, confirm staff_can_import_patrons is off
- Logout, login as staff1, visit /admin/patrons/import directly -> 403
- Login as admin, flip toggle on
- Logout, login as staff1
- /patrons should show the "Import CSV" button (it didn't before the
  toggle flipped)
- /admin/patrons/import is reachable
- /admin and /admin/settings still 403 for staff
- Staff can also reveal credentials: open Login Setup for any patron
  with a temp -> page renders, dismiss/regenerate work for staff role
- Login back as admin and disable the toggle to clean up

## 9. Force-change works for staff/admin too
```
sqlite3 data/database.sqlite "UPDATE users SET must_change_password=1 WHERE username='staff1';"
```
- Login as staff1 / Staff123! -> redirected to /account/change-password
- Set new password -> flag clears, normal access resumes

## 10. Regression sweep (quick)
- /catalog, book detail, book add/edit/delete still work
- /admin/backup export download still works
- Loan checkout/return still works
- /kiosk (logged out) still works

## After testing

Report any failures back to the conversation. Once green, the
remaining work is:
- Automated test pass (~35 tests planned across DB layer including the
  new temp-password methods, CSV parser, settings, change-password,
  import handlers, per-row reveal/dismiss/regenerate handlers)
- DEC-031 in DECISIONS.md (persist plaintext temp_password design)
- Schema doc update in docs/architecture.md
- Force-change subsection in docs/security.md
- README feature mention
- PR open referencing 8ap, rn0, d0w, fut as resolved
notes:
Manual testing in progress on csv-patron-import (origin/csv-patron-import @ d642e2b).

Sections passed:
- 1: admin tools-index nav (3 cards, back buttons)
- 2: settings page (toggle persists, flash banner)
- 3: records-only import (3 inserted, empty-name skipped, /patrons shows them with "no login")
- 4: dedup behavior (re-upload same CSV detects all as dups by email)
- 5: with-logins flow PARTIALLY tested -- preview rendered correctly with column mapping; result page UX gap discovered (see below)

Bug found and fixed mid-test (committed):
- Staff couldn't navigate to /admin/patrons/import even when toggle was on,
  because the entry point lived only on /admin (admin-only). Fix: added an
  "Import CSV" button on /patrons (admin always, staff when toggle on),
  removed the redundant card from /admin, made the Back-to-X button on the
  three import pages role-aware (admin -> /admin, staff -> /patrons).

Open design issue (UX hazard, not a bug):
- After a with-logins import, the result page shows ONE single-use
  download link for the credentials CSV. If the admin navigates away
  without clicking, the temp passwords are unrecoverable (bcrypt-hashed in
  DB, plaintext only lived in the in-memory token map under a URL the
  admin lost).
- Tim's preferred direction: show temp password inline per-patron on the
  /patrons table, not in a single dump on the result page.
- Three architectural shapes proposed:
  Shape 1: persist plaintext temp_password column on users; reveal-per-row
           UI; clear on first successful login or admin dismissal
  Shape 2: regenerate per-patron on demand (no plaintext stored, painful
           at scale)
  Shape 3: Shape 1 + AES encryption with app-key
- Three follow-up questions still to answer before locking design:
  - auto-clear on login only, or also admin-dismiss?
  - reveal visible to all staff or just admin?
  - allow regenerate-temp action for lost-temp recovery?
- Decision deferred -- tired. Will resume next session.

Sections still untested:
- 5 (need to re-test once design issue is resolved)
- 6: force-change-on-first-login flow (depends on a successful with-logins import)
- 7: force-change escape hatch
- 8: settings + role gating (full sweep)
- 9: force-change for staff/admin via sqlite manual flag
- 10: regression sweep

title: [CP7] Test coverage push (final session)
description:
Dedicated coverage session at the end of CP7, after #23 (admin panel ZIP export/import) and #24 (security hardening + deploy) have landed.

Why last: most of CP7 ships new code (admin handlers, security middleware, rate limiting, cache-busting). Doing the coverage push last means we cover that new code in the same session it gets reviewed for the security-review pass, rather than topping up coverage twice.

Baseline (2026-04-25): 61.8% total. Already-cleaned: LoadUser deleted as dead code (cs408-go-stack-3oj).

Scope:
- Open the HTML coverage view (go tool cover -html=coverage.out) and walk every red region in security-sensitive code: handlers_auth.go, handlers_*.go middleware-protected branches, db.go transactional methods (CheckoutBook, ReturnBook, UpdateUserPassword, CreatePatron, DeletePatron), validators.go.
- Fill the 0% Open Library trio: parsePublishYear (pure func, table test), normalizeOpenLibraryBook (feed canned response), FetchOpenLibraryBook (httptest.NewServer with stubbed JSON, plus error-path tests for 4xx/5xx/malformed).
- Fill 0% on covers.go:SaveCoverFromURL (httptest.NewServer returning real PNG) and the new admin export/import handlers (Zip Slip rejection test required).
- Add HandleBookNew test (currently 0%, GET /books/new with staff session asserts 200 + form fields).
- Boost partial-coverage handlers (HandleCatalog 50%, HandleMyLoans 50%, HandlePatronList 50%) -- the missing branches are typically empty-state and error paths.
- Skip (low signal): main.main, FetchAndStoreSeedCovers, extensionForImageMime.

Targets:
- Security-sensitive layers (auth, middleware, validators, transactional DB methods): 90%+
- Handlers: 80%+
- Overall: 75%+ (up from 61.8% baseline; admin panel and security middleware code adds new lines so the denominator grows during CP7)

Acceptance:
- go test ./... -cover prints >= 75% total
- go tool cover -func=coverage.out shows no 0% in handlers_auth.go, validators.go, openlibrary.go, covers.go (skipped exceptions documented in close notes)
- Zip Slip rejection test exists for the admin import path
- govulncheck output captured in close notes (paired with the supply-chain check from #24)

Sequencing: do NOT start until #23 and #24 have shipped their feature work. The 'finish CP7' session is the natural close-out point alongside the integration smoke.
notes:
Closed 2026-05-12. Coverage 67.6% (PR #76 baseline) -> 75.2% overall (74.9% libreshelf, 87.5% internal/safezip).

Approach: a header-gated fault-injection middleware appended to setupTestRouter's authenticated route groups (and the public-routes group for kiosk/login). Requests with X-Test-Break-Handler-DB: 1 get a closed *DatabaseManager swapped into the Gin context AFTER auth/CSRF/DBReadLock run, so middleware sees a healthy DB but the handler's first DB call returns "sql: database is closed" and the err != nil branch fires. No production Go was touched; main_test.go grew the brokenDM construction and the swap middleware (~15 lines), and handlers_coverage_test.go houses 50+ small per-branch tests.

Tests added across three categories:
1. Easy branches (no fault injection): invalid-ID parses, sql.ErrNoRows on the target, patron-without-login redirect, staff-with-import-setting canImport branch, patron-with-nil-PatronID 500 branch.
2. Handler DB-error fault injection via the new middleware (~20 handlers covered).
3. Validation rejection branches in HandleBookCreate / HandleBookUpdate (empty title, no authors, qty<1, year out of range, bad ISBN) and HandleStaffEdit (invalid username, duplicate username, missing target).
4. DB-method Begin-error branches: one test calls RegenerateTempPassword, DeleteUser, DeletePatron, UpdateBook, CreatePatron, CreateBook, UpdateUserPassword, ClearTempPassword, GetSetting, SetSetting on a closed-handle DM and asserts each errors.

Security-critical files now have no 0% functions:
- handlers_auth.go: all functions covered, lowest is HandleLoginPost at 74.3%
- validators.go: all >= 83.3%
- openlibrary.go: all >= 85.7%
- covers.go: all >= 66.7%

title: UX gap: both-sources-fail returns same 404 as both-sources-don't-have-the-book
description:
When OL has no record AND GB returns a real HTTP error (network/5xx/decode/auth), FetchOpenLibraryBook falls through to ErrOpenLibraryNotFound. The handler responds with 404 + {"error":"not_found"} and the JS shows 'no match in open library, fill in manually.'

From the admin's perspective, this is indistinguishable from 'neither source has the book' (both sources legitimately don't catalog the ISBN). The former is retryable -- GB might recover -- but the user sees the same UI either way.

Discovered during PR #81 manual test on 2026-05-16. Tim used 9780593875490 (Broken Dove, GB-only) with an invalid GB key. Got 'fill manually' instead of any banner indicating that GB tried and failed.

Possible fixes:

1. New error sentinel ErrExternalSourcesUnavailable. FetchOpenLibraryBook returns it when OL miss + GB real error. Handler renders 503 with body {"error":"external_sources_unavailable"} and JS shows 'External sources unavailable; try again or fill manually.'

2. Always return a BookPrefill (even empty) with GoogleBooksError=true when GB had a real error during OL-miss. Requires extending the handler's not-found-vs-success logic.

Lean toward option 1 -- cleaner error model, separates 'try again later' from 'fill manually permanently.'

Sequencing: low priority. Mechanism works for the common cases. File this so it doesn't get lost.

title: Seed-cover backfill consumes GB quota on fresh installs
description:
Final code review of feat/google-books-fallback (cs408-go-stack-8gj) flagged that FetchAndStoreSeedCovers in db.go calls FetchOpenLibraryBook directly. With Subproject A's parallel fan-out, every seed book with any OL gap will trigger a Google Books API call when GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_KEY is set.

On a fresh install with 100 seed books and an OL coverage rate well below 100% gaps-free, this could consume up to 100/1000 daily GB quota on the very first server start. After the first successful run, the SELECT in FetchAndStoreSeedCovers returns 0 rows (all seed books have covers persisted), so it's a one-time-per-fresh-install issue.

Practical exposure is narrow: most LibreShelf deployments only do this once per install, and operators with a 1000/day GB quota have headroom. But it's an unintended consumer of GB quota that the Subproject A spec didn't anticipate.

Three possible fixes:
1. New function FetchOpenLibraryBookOLOnly (or a bool param on FetchOpenLibraryBook) that skips the GB fan-out. FetchAndStoreSeedCovers uses the OL-only variant since it only consumes book.CoverURL.
2. Document the behavior in deployment.md and accept it.
3. Move the GB fan-out gate-check down INSIDE FetchOpenLibraryBook to also check 'is the caller interested in GB-sourced data?' (could be inferred from the context or a new param).

Option 1 is the cleanest. Add this as a small follow-up after Subproject A merges if Tim wants to tighten it.

Sequencing: low priority, NOT a blocker for Subproject A merge. P3.
notes: Resolved 2026-05-16 by reducing seed book count from 100 to 10. With 10 seed books, fresh-install GB quota consumption is at most 10/1000 daily (vs the prior 100/1000). Tim made the call after CS408 submission: 'demo doesn't need many books in the library.' Smaller seed is more honest about LibreShelf's small-library framing too. Tests still pass (handlers_kiosk_test and handlers_books_test depend only on Pride and Prejudice being book #1, which is preserved).

title: OL Lookup: parallelize work-fetch and jscmd=data-fetch fallbacks
description: The OL chain in FetchOpenLibraryBook currently runs fully sequentially. For sparse editions (Dell paperbacks etc.), this is 4 round-trips one-after-another: primary jscmd=details, then work, then ISBN-cover HEAD, then jscmd=data. Steps 2 (work fetch) and 4 (jscmd=data) are both *triggered* by emptiness in the primary response but are independent of each other -- the work fetch supplies description+cover-IDs, the data fetch supplies authors. They can run in parallel via goroutines, shaving ~300ms off the sparse-edition latency (worst case ~1100ms -> ~700ms). The cover HEAD probe still depends on the work fetch's result (only fires when work also has no covers) so stays sequential after step 2. Cost: ~50 lines of sync.WaitGroup plumbing. ROI: noticeable for the manual Lookup click on sparse editions; minimal for happy-path or seed-backfill.

title: OL Lookup: description doesn't update on a second consecutive lookup
description: When staff does an OL Lookup, all fields prefill. Doing a second lookup with a different ISBN updates title/authors/year/publisher/cover but NOT description -- it stays from the first lookup. Cause: the JS prefill block I added gates the description assignment on '!descriptionField.value.trim()' to avoid clobbering staff in-progress edits, but that gate also blocks the second-lookup case. The other fields don't have this gate; they overwrite unconditionally when OL returns a value. Fix: drop the guard so description follows the same overwrite-on-OL-hit semantics as the other fields.

title: OL Lookup: follow work record for covers too, not just descriptions
description: FetchAndStoreSeedCovers misses covers for sparse editions like Penguin Classics' Jane Eyre (ISBN 9780141441146). The edition record has covers=None but the work record OL1095427W has five cover IDs. Symptom: 'no cover URL from OL for ISBN X' in the seed backfill log for ~N books on a fresh install. Same pattern as the description bug just shipped in cs408-go-stack-dy3 -- OL splits cover data between editions and works, and we only read the edition. Fix: refactor fetchOLWorkDescription -> fetchOLWork returning both description and covers; use the work's first cover ID when the edition has none. Single HTTP call to the work, fills in either or both.
notes:
Closed 2026-05-13. Seed cover backfill went from 98/100 to 100/100 after this fix.

Two layers added to the cover-fallback chain on FetchOpenLibraryBook:

1. Work-record covers. fetchOLWorkDescription -> fetchOLWork now also returns []int Covers; when the edition's covers array is empty but a works ref is present, we use the work's first cover ID (via the existing olCoverURLTemplate /b/id/X-L.jpg builder). Same HTTP call we already made for descriptions, so no additional latency.

2. ISBN-based cover endpoint. OL has duplicate work records for some books -- the edition's linked work may have no covers even when a sibling work record has plenty (Tim's example: Charlotte's Web ISBN 9780064400558 points at OL44714598W which has covers=None, but the OL UI shows a cover and the canonical work OL483391W has cover_i=8461797). OL's covers.openlibrary.org/b/isbn/<isbn>-L.jpg endpoint resolves a cover whenever ANY work or edition has one indexed under that ISBN. We HEAD-probe with ?default=false (so missing returns 404 instead of a 1x1 placeholder) before committing the URL into the prefill payload -- this keeps broken images out of the staff form preview.

The remaining cases OL truly has nothing for (Atomic Habits in our seed) are still empty, but Atomic Habits ALSO got covered by the ISBN probe (302 from OL) -- so all 100 seed books now have local covers in data/covers/ after the backfill.

Storage is local. The CoverURL in the JSON response is transient; SaveCoverFromURL downloads to data/covers/<hash>.jpg and persists only the filename in the DB. The book detail page serves covers from local disk via Gin's static handler -- no network needed at render time, app works offline post-ingest. Confirmed by listing data/covers/ after the backfill: 100 jpgs averaging ~50KB each.

Tests: TestFetchOpenLibraryBook_FallsBackToWorkCovers, TestFetchOpenLibraryBook_FallsBackToISBNCoverProbe, TestFetchOpenLibraryBook_ISBNCoverProbeMissingStaysEmpty, TestFetchOpenLibraryBook_ISBNCoverProbeSkippedWhenEditionHasCover. The fake OL router gained a /b/isbn/ path that returns 200 for ISBNs in a configured set and 404 otherwise, plus openLibraryCoversHost is now swappable.

Live verification: full 100/100 seed cover recovery in 1m10s on a fresh DB (was 98/100 before). The two prior stragglers (9780735211292 Atomic Habits, 9780064400558 Charlotte's Web) both succeed via the new ISBN probe.

Security review: clean. The new URL construction goes through fmt.Sprintf with a hardcoded openLibraryCoversHost prefix and a stripped-ISBN integer-ish path segment; URL injection or SSRF gated by hard-exclusion #13 (path-only).

title: OL Lookup: handle the singular 'author' field and Last-First-dates format
description: HandleOpenLibraryLookup populates authors for some books (Dune) but not others (Pride and Prejudice). Cause: OL's jscmd=details returns authors under two different field names depending on the record. Edition records sometimes have authors: [{key, name}] (objects, 'First Last') and sometimes have author: ["Last, First, year-year."] (strings, last-first with dates). My olBook reads only the first shape. Fix: also read the singular 'author' field as []string; normalize each entry by stripping trailing period, splitting on first comma, dropping date suffix, and rebuilding as 'First Last'.

title: OL Lookup pre-fills description from jscmd=details
description: HandleOpenLibraryLookup currently uses jscmd=data which does not return descriptions. OpenLibraryBook has no Description field. When staff click 'Lookup ISBN' on the Add Book form, the description field stays blank and they type a short blurb by hand. Switch to jscmd=details, add Description to OpenLibraryBook (parsing OL's string-or-{type,value} shape), and update the JS-side prefill to populate the textarea. Lays groundwork for the Wikipedia fallback (cs408-go-stack-g5x).
notes:
Closed 2026-05-13. OL Lookup now pre-fills the description textarea on the Add Book / Edit Book form when OL has one for the ISBN.

Implementation:
- openlibrary.go: switched API call from jscmd=data to jscmd=details, restructured response types to match the new envelope (olEntry wrapping olBook under .details). Added Description field to OpenLibraryBook with a custom olDescription.UnmarshalJSON tolerating OL's two shapes: bare string (older records) and {type, value} typed-text object (newer records). Also tolerates null/number/malformed without erroring so a single bad record cannot block the whole lookup.
- Cover URL handling also changed: the details endpoint returns cover IDs as []int rather than pre-formatted URLs, so normalize now builds the URL from olCoverURLTemplate ("https://covers.openlibrary.org/b/id/%d-L.jpg"). Always requests -L for highest resolution.
- static/javascripts/app.js: added book-description field reference and a prefill block that only writes when the existing textarea is blank, so we never clobber a description the staff member is already in the middle of editing.

Tests added:
- TestOLDescriptionUnmarshal: six cases for the string/object/null/number/empty/object-with-empty-value shapes.
- TestFetchOpenLibraryBook_DescriptionAsBareString: end-to-end via httptest.NewServer for the older bare-string record shape.
- Existing TestNormalizeOpenLibraryBook expanded with description + cover-id assertions; old cover.small/medium/large preference tests removed (jscmd=details returns IDs not preformatted URLs).
- Existing TestFetchOpenLibraryBook_Success body updated to the new envelope.

Live verification (dev server on :3034, real OL API):
- ISBN 9780141439518 (Pride and Prejudice, Penguin Classics ed.) -> OL returns a full back-cover blurb ending in "--back cover".
- ISBN 9780441013593 (Dune) -> OL has no description; the field stays blank. This is the case g5x (Wikipedia fallback) addresses next.

Security review: clean. The new external description data flows through .value assignment (textContent-equivalent, not innerHTML), parameterized SQL storage, and html/template auto-escape on render. No new XSS, no new SSRF (cover URL is a static format string interpolated only with an integer ID), no auth boundary change.

Sets up cs408-go-stack-g5x: Wikipedia synopsis fallback for the books OL doesn't have descriptions for.

title: Concurrent checkout test (proves TOCTOU safety under load)
description:
Add a concurrency test that proves CheckoutBook is safe when N goroutines race to check out the last copy of a single-quantity book.

Why: the design is TOCTOU-safe (read+write inside one transaction, SQLite serializes writes via journal lock), but the suite has no test that actually exercises it under contention. db_loans_test.go:97 (TestCheckoutBookNoCopiesAvailable) only proves the guard returns ErrNoCopiesAvailable when quantity_available=0 sequentially -- it does not prove the guard holds under concurrent access.

The current closest test is sequential. A real test would catch any future regression where someone moves a guard outside the transaction.

Scope:
- New test in db_loans_test.go: TestCheckoutBookConcurrentRace (or similar).
- Setup: 1 book with quantity_available=1, 10 patrons (so the loan-limit guard does not interfere).
- Spawn N=10 goroutines, each calling CheckoutBook on that book with a different patron.
- sync.WaitGroup to join; channel to collect (success_count, ErrNoCopiesAvailable_count, other_err_count).
- Assert: exactly 1 success, exactly 9 ErrNoCopiesAvailable, 0 other errors.
- Assert: final quantity_available == 0 (NOT -9 -- the regression we are guarding against).
- Assert: COUNT(*) FROM loans == 1.

Caveat to verify before writing:
- modernc.org/sqlite uses a single connection by default. Confirm the test connection has _busy_timeout set high enough (or PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL) so concurrent transactions queue cleanly instead of returning SQLITE_BUSY. If not, the test will be flaky and the fix might be to set busy_timeout in the DSN, which is a small but real change to db.go connection setup.

Acceptance:
- Test passes 100x in a row under 'go test -count=100 -race ./...'
- Test added to coverage: visible in 'go test -v -run TestCheckoutBookConcurrent'

Sequencing: schedule alongside or just before cs408-go-stack-al3 (CP7 test coverage push). Pairs naturally with the security-review pass on CP7, and gives a defensible 'design verified under contention' talking point for CP6 demo retrospective.

Origin: raised during CP6 demo prep on 2026-04-26 -- demo script claims TOCTOU safety; honest review found no test that proves it.
notes:
Closed 2026-05-12. Shipped TestCheckoutBookConcurrentRace in db_loans_test.go.

Approach: N=10 goroutines, each calling CheckoutBook on a single book (quantity_available=1) with a distinct patron (to keep the loan-limit guard out of the way). Goroutines line up on a closed channel before firing so they race for real. Counters use atomic.Int32; the first non-ErrNoCopiesAvailable error is captured under a mutex for diagnostics.

Assertions:
- successCount == 1
- ErrNoCopiesAvailable count == 9
- other-error count == 0
- final quantity_available == 0 (NOT negative -- the regression we guard against)
- COUNT(*) FROM loans WHERE book_id = X == 1

Verified: passes 100x in a row under `go test -race -count=100 ./...`. Full suite (`go test ./... -race`) also green at 191s.

Caveat confirmed during implementation: the test originally failed under contention with "database is locked (5) (SQLITE_BUSY)" because the default SQLite busy_timeout is 0 -- the loser bounces off the journal lock instead of queueing. Fix shipped in the same commit: added PRAGMA busy_timeout = 5000 to openDB in db.go (after the existing WAL PRAGMA). 5s is well above any real LibreShelf transaction time and below any user-perceptible timeout. Verified load-bearing by temporarily disabling the PRAGMA and re-running -- the test fails with SQLITE_BUSY on multiple goroutines.

Security review (per CLAUDE.md session-close protocol): clean -- the PRAGMA value is a hardcoded literal with no user-input path; the change strengthens TOCTOU safety without introducing new attack surface.

title: Out-of-Stock catalog filter so dashboard card can link
description: The Out-of-Stock dashboard card on staff/admin home is currently non-clickable because /catalog has no server-side or client-side filter for quantity_available=0. Add an ?out=1 (or similar) query param + matching client-side toggle in app.js so the card can link to /catalog?out=1 and show only books that are completely checked out. Pairs with the broader catalog pagination work (Path 2 AJAX fragment swap) deferred from CP6 -- if that ships first, fold this filter in there.
notes:
Closed 2026-05-12. Filter shipped as /catalog?filter=out (matches the /loans?filter=overdue|active pattern, not the issue's suggested ?out=1).

Implementation:
- db.go: new GetOutOfStockBooks() -- mirror of GetAllBooks with WHERE b.quantity_available = 0 and the same authors GROUP_CONCAT shape.
- handlers_books.go: HandleCatalog now reads c.Query("filter") and dispatches via switch. Only "out" is recognized; any other value falls through to "" + GetAllBooks(). The normalized filter is passed to the template.
- templates/catalog.html: when Filter == "out", render a Bootstrap info banner ("Showing out of stock only (N books)") with a "Show all books" link back to /catalog.
- templates/index.html: dashboard Out-of-Stock card is now wrapped in <a href="/catalog?filter=out"> matching the Overdue / Active Loans card pattern above it.

Skipped the issue's "client-side toggle in app.js" idea -- one binary filter doesn't justify JS; the template's clear-filter link is simpler. The broader catalog AJAX pagination (cs408-go-stack-3d0) is still open and can fold this filter into a richer toolbar later if it ships.

Tests:
- TestGetOutOfStockBooksReturnsOnlyZeroAvailable -- four-book seed (two OOS, one partial-stock, one fully stocked); asserts only the two OOS books are returned in title order.
- TestGetOutOfStockBooksEmptyResult -- single fully-stocked book, asserts empty slice (no sql.ErrNoRows wrapper).
- TestCatalogFilterOutShowsOnlyOutOfStock -- handler-level, asserts /catalog?filter=out body contains the OOS title and NOT the in-stock title, plus the banner text.
- TestCatalogNoFilterShowsAllBooks -- companion default-branch test.

Manual UI check: dev server on :3033, logged in as admin, dashboard renders "Out of Stock 0" card wrapped in /catalog?filter=out link; filtered view renders banner "Showing out of stock only (0 books)".

Security review: clean. Whitelist switch on the filter param, hardcoded SQL (no string interpolation), Go html/template auto-escape on the only interpolated value (an integer count).

title: [Backlog] Docker container deployment option
description:
Optional alternative to the existing EC2 + systemd + nginx deployment path. Not replacing the current setup -- adding a parallel option for clients who prefer containerized deploys.

When un-deferred:

**Artifacts to build**
- Dockerfile (probably multi-stage: golang builder -> minimal runtime, FROM scratch or alpine since modernc.org/sqlite is pure Go with no CGo)
- docker-compose.yml example with SQLite volume mount, covers volume mount, env vars, port mapping
- New docs/docker.md walking through build + run, or a section in docs/deployment.md

**Env vars to surface** (already supported by the app, just need documentation in the container context)
- PORT, DATA_DIR, DB_NAME, ADMIN_PASSWORD
- LIBRESHELF_OFFLINE (DEC-034) for offline-locked deployments
- GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_KEY (DEC-035) optional

**Things to think through when designing**
- Data persistence: data/database.sqlite + WAL files via volume mount. Need to confirm WAL works correctly with bind mounts on macOS (some weirdness historically with Docker Desktop + SQLite).
- Cover storage: data/covers/ via volume mount too.
- Backup/restore: the existing ZIP export/import (DEC-027) writes through a swap pattern under a global RWMutex. Need to verify the file ops work inside a container with the data dir mounted.
- HTTPS: same constraint as today. Container should expose plain HTTP; client puts Caddy / nginx / Traefik in front for TLS termination.
- Multi-arch build? amd64 + arm64 covers the realistic deployment targets including Raspberry Pi / Apple Silicon dev.
- Where to publish: Docker Hub, GHCR, or just ship the Dockerfile and let the user build. For a "depends on the client" use case, shipping just the Dockerfile is the simplest. Publishing a public image opts into ongoing maintenance.

**Pairs with**
- cs408-go-stack-ij9 (GitHub Actions deploy automation) -- if the CI pipeline can build and push the image, that would be the natural place to do it.

Motivation: optional path for clients who already standardize on Docker. Not expected to be the primary deployment.

title: Restore black cover background for real covers; keep gray for no-cover placeholder
description: Per Tim's preference: real cover images should letterbox against a near-black slot (#212529, the pre-2026-05-03 behavior), but the no-cover SVG placeholder should keep its current soft-gray slot (#f0f4f8) so it blends with the SVG's own fill. Use CSS :has(img[src*='no-cover.svg']) to differentiate -- this also covers the onerror=swap-to-svg fallback case automatically since the selector tracks current src not initial state.

title: [Backlog] Wikipedia description fallback (tier-1 after OL)
description: Mirrored from closed GitHub #71. Replaces the closed Google Books description fallback (#68). Pulls the lead section of a book's Wikipedia article when OL returns a thin or empty description. Uses Wikipedia REST /api/rest_v1/page/summary/<Title> with disambiguation via opensearch. Full design in closed GitHub #71.
notes:
Closed 2026-05-13. Wikipedia synopsis fallback shipped, fires only when OL's description is empty or under 80 chars.

Implementation:
- wikipedia.go (new): FetchWikipediaSummary(ctx, title, authors) calls the Wikipedia REST /api/rest_v1/page/summary/<title> endpoint first. On disambiguation/404/author-mismatch, falls back to /w/api.php?action=opensearch with "<title> <first author>" and walks up to 5 candidates. Identifies as LibreShelf via User-Agent per Wikipedia's API policy.
- Author-match guard (extractMatchesAuthor): case-insensitive substring match on extract+description against any supplied author. Rejects results that don't mention the book's author -- guards against latching onto an unrelated topic with a similar title (e.g. "Dune" landform vs. novel). Requires at least one author for the opensearch path; without one, gives up rather than guess.
- handlers_books.go: HandleOpenLibraryLookup invokes the Wikipedia client when OL's description is < 80 chars (minDescriptionLengthForOL). On success, sets book.Description to the Wikipedia extract and book.DescriptionSource = "wikipedia". OL-good cases get DescriptionSource = "openlibrary". Wikipedia network failures are non-fatal -- the OL prefill still returns either way.
- openlibrary.go: OpenLibraryBook grows DescriptionSource field so JS can show the right banner.
- static/javascripts/app.js: status banner now branches on description_source -- "Prefilled from Open Library (description via Wikipedia). Review before saving." when Wikipedia provided the description.

Attribution: kept lightweight per design discussion -- just the form-status banner. No persisted source column, no embedded "via Wikipedia" suffix in the description text. Staff edits and submits; the resulting description is owned by staff.

Tests:
- wikipedia_test.go: direct title hit, author-mismatch rejection, disambiguation -> opensearch walk, opensearch walks past irrelevant hits to find author match, 404 returns empty, empty title returns empty, no-authors -> no opensearch fallback. Plus extractMatchesAuthor table tests (case sensitivity, partial last-name not matching full-name author, any-author-can-match).
- handlers_books_test.go: TestOpenLibraryLookup_FallsBackToWikipediaWhenOLDescriptionEmpty (full integration with both fakes), TestOpenLibraryLookup_OLDescriptionPreservedWhenLongEnough (Wikipedia must NOT clobber a good OL blurb -- pinned with an explicit "WIKI_OVERRIDE_TEXT" sentinel so a regression that wrongly invokes Wikipedia is visible).

Live verification against real OL + real Wikipedia (dev server on :3035):
- ISBN 9780441013593 (Dune) -> OL had no description; Wikipedia returned the full novel synopsis "Dune is a 1965 epic science fiction novel by American author Frank Herbert... It is one of the world's best-selling science fiction novels." with description_source = "wikipedia". Author-match guard passed on "Frank Herbert" in the extract.
- ISBN 9780141439518 (Pride and Prejudice, Penguin Classics ed.) -> OL's back-cover blurb preserved untouched, description_source = "openlibrary". Wikipedia was never invoked because OL's description was already long enough.

Security review: clean. Hardcoded base URLs (host can't be controlled), url.PathEscape on title (no SSRF), 10s per-request timeout, plain-text extract flows through textContent assignment + parameterized SQL + html/template auto-escape. The author-match guard is data-quality, not a security control.

Next in the chain: cs408-go-stack-fcb (placeholder SVG cover), cs408-go-stack-069 (OL endpoint exhaustion), cs408-go-stack-l2h (IA covers), cs408-go-stack-8gj (Google Books). 4xq (jscmd=details) and g5x complete the description side; the rest of the open issues are cover-side.

title: [Backlog] Google Books API as fallback after OL ISBN miss
description:
Add Google Books API as a higher-coverage fallback when the Open Library ISBN endpoint returns no result, before falling through to the OLID/LCCN/OCLC paths in #70 or the Internet Archive cover path in #72. OL stays primary so the open-data alignment of the project is preserved.

Origin: 2026-04-26 -- a CS408 classmate's project demoed Google Books for book lookup and the coverage and metadata quality were noticeably better than what we get from OL alone, especially for newer titles. Filed for post-submission consideration.

Why fallback, not replacement:
- Keeps OL as the open-data primary, which lines up with 'self-hostable library' framing.
- Slots cleanly into the existing OL-fallback chain (cs408-go-stack-* mirrors of GH #70 OL endpoint exhaustion, #71 Wikipedia descriptions, #72 IA covers).
- A single coherent Google Books query by ISBN often beats running OL through OLID/LCCN/OCLC paths -- so this might make #70 partially redundant. Worth re-evaluating #70 scope after this lands.

Where it sits in the chain (proposed):
1. OL ISBN endpoint (current)
2. **Google Books by ISBN (this issue)**
3. OL OLID/LCCN/OCLC for covers (#70)
4. Internet Archive covers (#72)
5. Wikipedia descriptions (#71) -- runs in parallel for description-only enrichment
6. Placeholder cover generator (#65) -- last resort

Scope:
- New file googlebooks.go mirroring shape of openlibrary.go.
- FetchGoogleBooksByISBN(isbn string) (*Book, error) returning the same normalized Book struct used by openlibrary normalizer so the integration is drop-in.
- API key in env var GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_KEY -- OPTIONAL. If unset, fallback is skipped silently (log once at startup that the fallback is disabled). Do not error or refuse to start.
- Wire into the admin OL Lookup handler (handlers_books.go HandleOpenLibraryLookup) and into the seed-cover backfill in main.go (FetchAndStoreSeedCovers) so both UI lookup and bulk import benefit.
- Respect 1000/day free-tier limit: log a warning when daily request count exceeds, say, 800 so the operator knows to either rotate to a paid key or back off.
- Cache responses in-process for the lifetime of a request? -- decide during implementation. Probably not needed for the admin lookup path (one ISBN per click), but the backfill loop should de-dupe before calling.

Tests:
- httptest.NewServer with stubbed Google Books responses for: happy path with cover, happy path without cover, no-results, 4xx, 5xx, malformed JSON, rate-limit (429).
- Unit test for the 'GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_KEY missing -> skip silently' branch.
- Integration test for the chained fallback: stub OL to return empty, stub Google Books to return a hit, assert the final Book has Google Books data.

ToS / compliance:
- Google Books API ToS allows caching with attribution. Re-read https://developers.google.com/books/terms before deciding to store covers locally. The current cover storage pattern (download to data/covers/) is probably fine but verify -- attribution might mean a small 'Cover via Google Books' note on the book detail page, depending on the exact terms at the time of implementation.

Operational:
- API key rotation: document that setting GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_KEY=<new> and restarting the service is sufficient. No DB migration needed.
- API key in deploy/env.example or equivalent so future deploys know the var exists.

Acceptance:
- Setting GOOGLE_BOOKS_API_KEY in env enables the fallback. Unsetting disables it cleanly.
- An ISBN that OL doesn't know but Google Books does returns a populated Book on admin Lookup.
- govulncheck and 'go test ./...' both pass.
- DECISIONS.md gets a new entry recording the tier order and the open-data-primary reasoning.

Sequencing: post-submission. Do AFTER the CP7 close-out and the OL chain decisions are revisited. Possibly partially supersedes #70 -- evaluate together.
notes:
Merged via PR #81 on 2026-05-16. Subproject A complete: Google Books fallback + enrichment shipped with the OL chain. 12 commits TDD-ordered. Manual test plan completed by Tim, including:
- Test 2 (sparse partial-OL + GB enrichment): verified via GB API traffic dot
- Test 4 (GB error path): mechanism unit-tested; manual repro hard because OL improved Rule of Four edition data since the bd memory was written
- Test 5 (offline mode beats GB): verified
- Test 6 (kiosk attribution): three footer branches all confirmed
- Test 7 (JSON shape): both source-label branches verified via Broken Dove and Rule of Four

Two follow-ups still pending: cs408-go-stack-lh0 already fixed in this PR (close on merge); UX gap when both OL miss + GB error returns same 404 as both-sources-don't-have-it (file as follow-up). DEC-035 published. Subproject A closes the multi-PR Google Books integration sequence.

title: [Backlog] Server-side catalog pagination -- Path 2 (AJAX fragment swap)
description:
Mirrors GitHub #37 (which originally captured a v2 server-side-with-form-submit design that was downgraded UX). Path 2 is the right approach: AJAX fragment swap preserves the live-filter feel of the current client-side render while gaining true server-side pagination at scale.

Why deferred: the current all-in-DOM catalog render at 100 seeded books filters instantly via client-side JS and the UX is genuinely good. Server-side with full-page form submit (v2 shape) was a UX downgrade. Unblocked when catalog routinely exceeds ~500 rows or when post-submission session frees up.

Path 2 design:

Templates: extract catalog grid + pagination controls into a named {{define "catalog_results"}} template block.

Handler: HandleCatalog branches on X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest (or ?partial=1) to render just that block on AJAX requests, full layout on direct loads.

Frontend (app.js): debounces the search input (~300ms), genre/available change events fire immediately, each interaction calls fetch() with the AJAX header, swaps #catalog-results innerHTML, updates URL via history.replaceState (search keystrokes) or pushState (discrete state changes), uses AbortController to prevent stale-wins races.

No-JS fallback: full-page form submit. Same handler renders the full template when not asked for the partial.

Fragment pattern reuses for /loans filtering if that page ever outgrows all-in-DOM.

DB layer when built:
- ListBooks(filter, page, pageSize) (BookPage, error)
- GetAllGenres() ([]string, error)
- count query sharing the same WHERE clause
- Author search via correlated EXISTS subquery to avoid fighting the GROUP_CONCAT aggregate currently used in the catalog query

Pagination widget: numbered pages with ellipsis, 24 per page.

Full design preserved in docs/cp6-planning.md under 'Catalog Pagination (Path 2: AJAX fragment swap)'.

title: [Backlog] Offline cover sync via flash drive (3-machine workflow)
description: Air-gap workflow for fetching Open Library covers when the LibreShelf server has no internet access. Designed for the same restricted-network persona as cs408-go-stack-o1x (self-reg toggle off): prison libraries, secure facilities, anywhere policy or topology blocks outbound HTTP from the production box. Today, a book row created without a cover stays coverless forever on an offline install -- the only way to add covers is to manually upload one per book through the edit form. With many books, that is impractical. This feature creates a courier workflow where an admin uses a personal laptop with internet to ferry covers to the offline server via flash drive. Three machines, three artifacts: (1) offline server runs an export utility that scans the books table for rows missing a cover and writes a manifest JSON; admin copies to flash drive. (2) Connected machine runs a fetcher tool that reads the manifest, queries OL for each book, validates downloads via the existing covers.go magic-byte path, packages into a return ZIP. (3) Offline server admin uploads the ZIP, server validates Zip Slip + server_id match + per-cover MIME, writes files to DATA_DIR/covers/, updates books.cover_filename. Sequencing: depends on settings table from cs408-go-stack-o1x (for server_id), Zip Slip helper from #23 (CP7), and a refactor of openlibrary.go and covers.go into reusable internal/ packages so the connected fetcher binary can import them without dragging in the web app. Cross-refs cs408-go-stack-zcs (offline detection on lookup button -- adjacent connectivity feature) and #23 (admin ZIP export/import -- reuses Zip Slip helper).

title: [Backlog] Patron self-registration toggle (admin-controlled)
description:
Admin-only setting on /admin that gates patron self-registration. Default OFF: only staff and admin can create patron accounts (current CP5 behavior, suitable for restricted environments like prisons or correctional facilities where inmates must not self-register). When toggled ON: a public /register route is exposed that lets a patron create their own account.

Why this matters: LibreShelf's design assumes a small library context, but the deployment environment changes the trust model. A public library wants the friction of self-service signup; a prison library cannot allow it. Making this a runtime toggle (not a build flag) lets the same binary serve both deployments.

Design questions when un-deferred:

Settings storage. Add a settings table (key TEXT PRIMARY KEY, value TEXT, updated_at TEXT, updated_by INTEGER FK users.id) rather than a one-row config blob, so future toggles slot in cleanly. Cache in memory at startup with a write-through invalidate on update. Default-deny: missing key reads as OFF.

Admin UI. New section on /admin (admin-only, already gated). Bootstrap toggle switch with a confirmation modal on flip (Are you sure you want to allow patrons to create their own accounts?). Server records updated_by + updated_at for audit. Flash code on success.

Public registration route. New GET/POST /register handler in the public route group (no auth middleware). On every request: short-circuit 404 or redirect to /login if the setting is OFF, so the route does not advertise itself when disabled. CSRF via the existing pre-session double-submit cookie pattern (same shape as login). Rate limit at the middleware layer when CP7 rate limiting lands -- flag this as an integration point.

Patron creation path. Reuse the CreatePatron transactional flow (patrons + users) but with the username chosen by the user rather than auto-generated by generateBaseUsername. Add a username uniqueness check before commit (the existing collision-retry loop is for auto-generation; user-chosen usernames need explicit validation). Password rules: minimum length, bcrypt cost matches existing. No email verification in v1 -- add as a follow-up if abuse becomes real. No admin approval queue in v1 -- the toggle itself is the gate.

Auto-login on success or redirect to login? Probably auto-login + flash welcome -- matches public expectations and avoids a confusing extra step. Decide at design time.

Sidebar and login page. When the setting is ON, add a Create an account link below the Sign In button on the login page (mirror placement of the kiosk link added in cs408-go-stack-qqx). Conditionally rendered server-side based on the setting.

Audit and rollback. Include the toggle event in any future audit log work. Flipping the setting OFF does not delete already-self-registered accounts -- they become indistinguishable from staff-created patrons.

Testing. Unit tests for the setting read/write path. Handler tests for /register in both states (OFF returns 404, ON happy path, ON with duplicate username, ON with weak password). Boundary test: confirm /register is not reachable when OFF even with CSRF and form data set up correctly.

title: [Backlog] Offline detection for the Open Library Lookup button
description:
When the user's browser can't reach OL, hide or disable the Lookup button on the New/Edit book form rather than leaving it to always-fails-until-timeout. Today the button is unconditionally visible and clicking it when offline yields a slow timeout error rather than a clear up-front signal.

Plan is A + D combined:

A. navigator.onLine + online/offline event listeners. Handles the unplugged-laptop and airplane-mode cases synchronously and for free. Listen on window.online and window.offline, hide/show the button accordingly. Initial state set from navigator.onLine on page load.

D. On the first OL fetch failure within a session, set a session-scoped flag (sessionStorage) that hides the Lookup button for subsequent form opens in the same session and surfaces 'Open Library unreachable. Fill in manually.' inline next to where the button used to be. Flag persists for the browser session, gets cleared on tab close so the next session retries OL fresh.

All in app.js. No server changes. No probe endpoint.

Known gap: a network that has general internet but blocks openlibrary.org specifically (corporate firewall, DNS block) still shows the button on first attempt -- acceptable because D catches it on click with a clear message and hides the button for the rest of the session.

If we later want stronger 'actually reachable from EC2' detection (e.g. corporate networks where the server has internet but OL is blocked downstream), layer on a server-side probe at startup or cached per-N-minutes that renders the form with an OpenLibraryReachable boolean flag in the template data. Out of scope for v1.

From #20.

title: [Backlog] Orphan cover cleanup on post-cover-save validation failure
description: HandleBookCreate and HandleBookUpdate save the cover to disk BEFORE the duplicate-ISBN pre-check and DB write. If a cover-save succeeds but a later step fails (duplicate ISBN, transient DB error), the file is orphaned under data/covers/. Low-frequency leak in practice since duplicates are caught client-side before submit in the common case. Two fix paths: (a) stage the cover bytes in memory until the DB write succeeds, or (b) janitor pass that sweeps data/covers/ for files not referenced in books.cover_filename. Same issue exists in Create flow shipped in #20 and Update cover-replacement flow.

title: [Backlog] Password-reset Variant 2 (server-generated temp + force-change)

Requires: must_change_password column on users, login-middleware branch that redirects flagged users to a /change-password page, self-service password-change handler + template, one-shot display of the generated temp password to the admin. Variant 1 (admin-chosen password, current implementation) is acceptable for a small trusted staff; Variant 2 is the right long-term default. Applies to both staff and patron reset flows. From #39.

title: [Backlog] Staff/Patron/Book table responsive polish

New /my/holds page (mirrors /my/loans pattern, route in patron group). Notification on return: server pushes via SSE (see SSE backlog item) and/or shows a flash banner on next login. Originally part of CP6 #22, deferred post-submission with favorites and SSE.

title: [Backlog] SSE live availability updates
description: Server-Sent Events stream so a kiosk or catalog tab updates a book's availability badge in real time when staff checks the book in/out from another browser. Endpoint: GET /events. Subscribe pattern: clients open the stream and the server emits 'book_availability' events with {book_id, quantity_available} payloads on every successful CheckoutBook / ReturnBook. Single global stream is fine for CP6-scale; per-book channel is over-engineering. Replaces the original CP6 'kiosk live updates' scope which was deferred together with holds. Note rate limiting / connection caps if we ever add hold notifications on top.

title: [Backlog] Patron activate / deactivate (soft suspension)
description: Soft-deactivation flag on the patron record so admins can suspend a patron temporarily without destroying the row. Useful when (1) the patron has active loans blocking delete, or (2) the patron leaves the library for a stretch and may return. Estimated 1.5-2h: add patrons.is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT 1 (requires fresh DB since CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cannot ALTER), activate/deactivate handler pair (mirror staff reset-password shape), login middleware branch that rejects authentication for users whose linked patron is inactive, session wipe on deactivate (same idiom as UpdateUserPassword per DEC-022), Actions-column button that toggles state, 'inactive' badge on the patron row. Maps cleanly onto the existing flash system (patron_activated, patron_deactivated codes). Raised during #21 smoke test, deferred post-submission.

title: [Backlog] Patron metadata column UI

Future work: free-form notes textarea, OR structured fields (student ID, library card number, address, etc.) for libraries that want to track more than name/email/phone. UI shape probably an extra section on the patron edit modal. Cut from #21 to keep CP5 shippable on 4/24.

title: [Backlog] Patron reset-password handler + modal
description: Mirror the staff reset-password pattern (Variant 1 admin-typed). Today's recovery path is 'admin deletes the patron and recreates with a new password' which is acceptable for a small library but not great UX. Estimated work: one POST handler + one modal + ~3 tests. Variant 2 (server-generated temp + force-change-on-next-login) is the stronger long-term path, tracked in the staff Variant 2 backlog item. Cut from #21 to keep CP5 shippable on 4/24.
notes:
Real gap surfaced during 2026-05-17 manual testing of PR #85 (ygu): admin has no path to reset a patron's password after creation. Currently the only recovery path is "delete and recreate" which is unacceptable UX.

Design: copy the staff reset-password modal pattern from /staff. Variant 1 (admin-typed new password) is the simplest landing. Variant 2 (server-generated temp + force-change-on-first-login) is tracked separately at cs408-go-stack-rn0 but is the stronger long-term path -- the CSV-import path already has force-change scaffolding (DEC-030) that this could reuse.

Recommended sequencing: ship Variant 1 now since the staff pattern is right there to copy; consider Variant 2 as a follow-up once the immediate gap is closed.

title: Loan history view (staff per-patron, patron self-view, admin audit) -- deferred post-submission
description:
CP6 ships only active and overdue loan views. A complete history (returned loans included) is not surfaced anywhere except the per-book panel on the staff book-detail page.

Three audiences need it:
1. Staff/admin should be able to pull a patron's full history (e.g. 'this patron returns books late often').
2. Patrons should be able to see what they have read in the past, not just what they currently hold.
3. Admins running an audit should be able to filter by date range.

Design questions when un-deferred:

Route shape: /patrons/:id/history for staff vs /my/history for patrons -- separate routes preserve the privacy boundary built in CP6 around /loans vs /my/loans.

Endpoint approach: whether to add a ?include=returned flag on the existing endpoints or build a new pair. Separate routes likely cleaner because the privacy boundary is already enforced at the route group.

Pagination strategy: needed if loan rows grow past the all-in-DOM threshold. Same Path 2 (AJAX fragment swap) approach as the catalog pagination work would apply.

CSV export for the staff view: likely useful for audit purposes, slots into the CP7 admin panel pattern.

DB layer impact:
- GetActiveLoans / GetPatronActiveLoans currently filter on returned_at IS NULL -- preserve.
- New GetLoanHistory(patronID, ...) for staff per-patron view.
- New GetPatronFullHistory(patronID, ...) for /my/history.
- Count query for pagination, sharing the same WHERE clause.

Existing LoanListRow struct is reusable -- add returned_at as an optional/pointer field.

Templates:
Option A: extend loans.html and my_loans.html with a 'Show returned' toggle.
Option B: build a dedicated history page that includes a 'Returned on' column.
Lean toward B for cleaner mental separation between 'what's outstanding' (action-oriented) and 'what happened' (audit-oriented).

Privacy: avoid leaking other-patron history to a patron-role user via the same route-group enforcement pattern as /my/loans (RequirePatron middleware, *user.PatronID always used as the filter).

value: Feature branches always go through a GitHub pull request, never merged directly to main locally. Tim manually reviews and tests the PR before merging. Workflow per session close: (1) push the feature branch to origin, (2) gh pr create with a detailed body, (3) hand off to Tim. Don't run 'git checkout main && git merge' from the assistant side. The CLAUDE.md note that cp6-loans is an 'ephemeral branch (no upstream)' was per-session shorthand at branch creation time, not a directive to skip PRs -- branches still get pushed when the work is ready for review.

Significant PRs must include a Test plan section in the body covering: (1) what was tested automatically (go test ./..., go vet, gofmt scope), (2) what to test manually before merge (concrete browser/UI steps for any feature-flagged or behavior-changing work), (3) rollback notes if the change has any operational risk. Why: PR reviewers (including future Tim) need a checklist to confirm the merge is safe, not just a summary of what the code does. How to apply: every gh pr create command for feature work uses the HEREDOC template with ## Summary, ## Test plan (automatic), ## Test plan (manual), and ## Rollback or operational notes if applicable. Trivial PRs (docs-only typo fixes, comment edits) can skip the manual section.

Before closing a beads issue whose code changes touched handlers, DB methods, middleware, auth/session logic, templates rendering user data, or anything related to credentials/permissions, run the security-review skill (Skill tool with skill="security-review") on the branch diff. Address findings before close. Skip for docs-only, beads-only, README/CLAUDE.md, or test-only changes -- those don't introduce vulnerabilities. Mirrors the testing-discipline pattern. Established 2026-05-10 after the csv-patron-import branch review surfaced two real medium-severity issues (CSV formula injection in downloaded credentials CSV, missing Cache-Control: no-store on the credentials download response). Also documented in CLAUDE.md Session Completion step 3 and AGENTS.md Security Review Requirements.

Subagent model selection for subagent-driven-development on this project: implementer=Sonnet, spec compliance reviewer=Sonnet, code quality reviewer=Opus, final whole-branch reviewer=Opus. Why: plans contain near-complete code so implementer work is translation (Sonnet is enough). Spec compliance review is mechanical text-matching (Sonnet is enough). Code quality review needs architectural judgment to catch subtle issues (Opus is materially better -- Sonnet has been catching small stuff but missing nuances like SSRF, pattern divergence). Final whole-branch review needs cross-task synthesis (Opus). Established 2026-05-15 during Subproject A implementation after Tim noted he wanted to make sure the tool was being used optimally.
